metadata.title: AI Terminology Cheat Sheet
metadata.description: Quick-reference glossary of AI/LLM terms, organized using the same topic taxonomy as the quiz.

terms.definition: A neural network trained on massive text corpora to predict and generate language, powering chat assistants and copilots.
terms.example: GPT-4, Claude, and Llama are all LLMs.

terms.definition: The basic unit of text (a word, subword, or character piece) a model reads and generates. Context limits and API costs are measured in tokens.
terms.example: "unbelievable" might split into "un", "believ", "able".

terms.definition: The process of adjusting a model's internal parameters on data so it learns patterns, typically done via pretraining followed by fine-tuning.
terms.example: Pretrain on web text, then fine-tune on curated instructions.

terms.definition: Running an already-trained model to produce output for a given input, as opposed to training it.
terms.example: Sending a prompt to an API and getting a completion back.

terms.definition: When a model generates confident-sounding output that is factually wrong or entirely fabricated.
terms.example: Citing a research paper that doesn't actually exist.

terms.definition: The internal numeric weights a model learns during training; roughly indicates model scale and capacity.
terms.example: A "70B" model has about 70 billion parameters.

terms.definition: The maximum number of tokens (input plus output) a model can consider at one time.
terms.example: A 200K-token context window can hold roughly a 500-page book.

terms.definition: Controls used to pick the next token from the model's probability distribution; temperature adjusts how random that choice is.
terms.example: Temperature 0 gives deterministic, repeatable answers.

terms.definition: A dense numeric vector representing the meaning of text, used for search and similarity comparisons.
terms.example: "dog" and "puppy" have embeddings that sit close together.

terms.definition: Cached attention key/value tensors from prior tokens that let a model avoid recomputing them for every new token, speeding up generation.
terms.example: Long conversations reuse the cache instead of reprocessing all prior history.

terms.definition: Reducing the numeric precision of a model's weights (e.g. 16-bit to 4-bit) to shrink memory use and speed up inference, at some accuracy cost.
terms.example: Running a large model on a laptop GPU via 4-bit quantization.

terms.definition: The raw, unnormalized scores a model outputs for each possible next token before they are converted into probabilities.
terms.example: A softmax function turns logits into a probability distribution.

terms.definition: A mechanism that lets a model weigh how relevant every other token is when processing a given token.
terms.example: In "The cat sat because it was tired", attention links "it" to "cat".

terms.definition: Attention applied within a single sequence, letting every token attend to every other token in the same input.
terms.example: Resolving pronoun references across a paragraph.

terms.definition: Information added to token representations so the model knows word order, since attention alone is order-agnostic.
terms.example: RoPE (rotary positional encoding) is used in many modern LLMs.

terms.definition: An architecture where only a subset of specialized "expert" sub-networks activate per token, increasing capacity without a proportional compute cost.
terms.example: A model with 8 experts might route each token through only 2 of them.

terms.definition: The per-token fully-connected layer inside each transformer block that follows attention, adding non-linear processing capacity.
terms.example: Roughly two-thirds of a transformer's parameters typically live here.

terms.definition: A technique that rescales activations within a layer to stabilize and speed up training of deep networks.
terms.example: Applied before or after each attention/FFN sub-block.

terms.definition: Asking a model to perform a task from instructions alone, with no examples provided.
terms.example: "Translate this sentence to French."

terms.definition: Providing a handful of example input/output pairs in the prompt to steer the model's behavior before asking it to do the task.
terms.example: Three sample Q&A pairs followed by a new question.

terms.definition: Prompting a model to reason step-by-step before giving a final answer, which improves accuracy on multi-step problems.
terms.example: "Let's think step by step..."

terms.definition: A prompting pattern that interleaves reasoning ("Thought") with actions ("Act") like tool calls, then observes results before continuing.
terms.example: Thought → search the web → Observation → Answer.

terms.definition: A privileged instruction set at the start of a conversation that shapes a model's persona, rules, and constraints for the whole session.
terms.example: "You are a concise, professional support agent."

terms.definition: Constraining a model's response to a defined schema (like JSON) so downstream code can parse it reliably.
terms.example: Forcing a response to match { "name": string, "age": number }.

terms.definition: Fetching relevant external documents at query time and feeding them into the model's context so it can answer with up-to-date or private information.
terms.example: Searching a company wiki before answering an internal question.

terms.definition: Finding the most relevant documents by comparing embedding similarity rather than exact keyword matches.
terms.example: A query about "car trouble" also matches a document about "vehicle breakdown".

terms.definition: Splitting long documents into smaller passages before embedding and indexing them, since retrieval works on chunk-sized units.
terms.example: Splitting a 50-page PDF into 500-token chunks.

terms.definition: A second, more precise scoring pass over an initial set of retrieved candidates to reorder them by true relevance before they reach the model.
terms.example: A cross-encoder reranker re-scores the top 50 vector-search hits.

terms.definition: A datastore optimized for storing embeddings and running fast nearest-neighbor similarity search at scale.
terms.example: Pinecone, Weaviate, and pgvector are all vector databases.

terms.definition: Anchoring a model's answer in retrieved or provided source material so it is less likely to hallucinate.
terms.example: Requiring the model to quote the exact source passage it used.

terms.definition: A system where an LLM autonomously plans, calls tools, observes results, and iterates toward a goal rather than producing one-shot output.
terms.example: An agent that books a flight by searching, comparing, and confirming.

terms.definition: A model's ability to emit a structured request to invoke an external function or API, then incorporate the result into its response.
terms.example: Calling a get_weather(city) function instead of guessing.

terms.definition: The repeating cycle of think → act → observe that lets an agent make progress on a task across multiple steps.
terms.example: Search, read the result, decide the next search, repeat.

terms.definition: An open standard for connecting AI models to external tools, data sources, and systems through a common interface.
terms.example: One MCP server exposing both a database and a filesystem to any compatible client.

terms.definition: Multiple specialized agents (or agent instances) coordinating, delegating, or debating to solve a task none could solve as well alone.
terms.example: A "planner" agent delegating research to a "researcher" agent.

terms.definition: A structured test suite used to measure how well a model or agent performs a task, often used to catch regressions before shipping changes.
terms.example: Running 200 saved tasks against a new prompt to check the pass rate.

terms.definition: Deliberately curating exactly what information enters a model's limited context window, and in what order, to get better and cheaper results.
terms.example: Trimming irrelevant chat history before a new turn.

terms.definition: Summarizing or discarding older context to free up space in the context window as a conversation or task grows long.
terms.example: Replacing 50 old messages with a 3-sentence summary.

terms.definition: Fetching information only at the moment it is needed, rather than pre-loading everything upfront, to keep context lean.
terms.example: Looking up a file's contents only when a task actually references it.

terms.definition: Persistent notes or state an agent writes and reads across steps or sessions so it doesn't have to re-derive the same information.
terms.example: A running to-do list the agent updates as it completes steps.

terms.definition: The tendency for model performance to degrade as irrelevant or stale information accumulates in a long context window.
terms.example: An agent losing track of the original goal after 100 tool calls.

terms.definition: The portion of the context window deliberately allocated to a given kind of content (instructions, history, retrieved docs) so the most important information isn't crowded out.
terms.example: Capping retrieved documents to 2,000 tokens so instructions always fit.

terms.definition: Designing the tools, permissions, and feedback loops around an agent so it can act effectively and safely in its environment.
terms.example: Deciding which shell commands an agent is allowed to run.

terms.definition: The schema (name, description, parameters) that tells a model what a tool does and how to call it correctly.
terms.example: A well-written description prevents a model from misusing a delete_file tool.

terms.definition: The rules governing which actions an agent may take autonomously versus which require human approval.
terms.example: Auto-approving file reads but requiring confirmation before a git push.

terms.definition: The signal a harness gives an agent about the result of its actions (success, error, output) so it can adjust its next step.
terms.example: Returning a compiler error so the agent can fix its own code.

terms.definition: Running an agent's actions in an isolated environment so mistakes or malicious commands can't affect production systems.
terms.example: Executing generated code inside a disposable container.

terms.definition: Logging and tracing an agent's decisions, tool calls, and outputs so developers can debug and improve its behavior.
terms.example: A trace viewer showing every tool call an agent made during a task.

terms.definition: A training technique that uses human preference judgments to reward a model for outputs people rate as better, aligning it with human intent.
terms.example: Raters rank two model responses; the model is trained to prefer the higher-ranked one.

terms.definition: An alignment approach where a model critiques and revises its own outputs against a written set of principles, reducing reliance on human labeling for every case.
terms.example: A model checking its own answer against a rule like "avoid harmful instructions."

terms.definition: A prompt crafted to bypass a model's safety training and get it to produce disallowed content.
terms.example: Disguising a harmful request as a "fictional story".

terms.definition: An attack where malicious instructions are hidden in content a model processes (a webpage, document, email) to hijack its behavior.
terms.example: A webpage containing hidden text telling an AI assistant to leak private data.

terms.definition: Deliberately probing a model or system for weaknesses, harmful outputs, or exploitable behavior before it is deployed.
terms.example: A security team trying dozens of jailbreak prompts before launch.

terms.definition: Automated checks (filters, classifiers, rules) placed around a model to catch or block unsafe or off-policy outputs.
terms.example: A content filter blocking a response before it reaches the user.

terms.definition: An AI model that can process and/or generate more than one type of data, such as text, images, audio, or video.
terms.example: GPT-4o can accept text, images, and audio, and produce text or audio.

terms.definition: A model that connects visual and linguistic representations to perform tasks like image captioning or visual question answering.
terms.example: Describing what's happening in a photo in a full sentence.

terms.definition: A generative model that produces images (or other data) by iteratively removing noise from a random starting point, guided by a conditioning signal like a text prompt.
terms.example: Stable Diffusion turning a text prompt into a photorealistic image.

terms.definition: A model trained to align text and image embeddings in a shared vector space, enabling zero-shot image classification and guiding image generation.
terms.example: Matching the caption "a dog on a beach" to the right photo among thousands.

terms.definition: A transformer architecture that treats an image as a sequence of patches and applies self-attention, instead of using convolutions.
terms.example: Splitting a photo into a grid of small patches before feeding it to the model.

terms.definition: A model skill that converts spoken audio into a text transcript.
terms.example: Whisper transcribing a recorded meeting into text.

terms.definition: Systematic errors in a model's outputs that unfairly advantage or disadvantage particular groups, often reflecting biases in training data.
terms.example: A hiring model trained on historical data disadvantaging women applicants.

terms.definition: A facially neutral policy or practice that disproportionately harms a protected group, even without intent to discriminate.
terms.example: A loan-scoring rule that unintentionally denies a protected group at a higher rate.

terms.definition: The ability to understand and communicate why a model produced a particular output or decision.
terms.example: Highlighting which words in a resume most influenced a hiring model's score.

terms.definition: A formal criterion, such as demographic parity or equalized odds, used to check whether a model treats different groups equitably.
terms.example: Checking that a loan model approves applicants from different groups at similar rates.

terms.definition: The European Union's binding law that classifies AI systems by risk level and sets requirements scaled to that risk.
terms.example: Banning real-time biometric surveillance in public spaces for law enforcement, with narrow exceptions.

terms.definition: A mathematical guarantee that adding or removing any single record barely changes a model's output distribution, protecting individual privacy.
terms.example: Adding calibrated noise during training so no one person's data can be reverse-engineered.

terms.definition: Continuing to train a pre-trained model on a smaller, task- or domain-specific dataset to specialize its behavior.
terms.example: Taking a general model and further training it on customer-support transcripts.

terms.definition: A family of fine-tuning methods that train a small set of extra parameters while keeping most base-model weights frozen.
terms.example: Fine-tuning a 70B model by training only a few million adapter parameters.

terms.definition: A PEFT technique that injects small trainable low-rank matrices into a model's weight layers instead of updating the full weights.
terms.example: Adapting a model's style with a LoRA file a few hundred megabytes in size.

terms.definition: Fine-tuning a base model on (instruction, response) pairs so it reliably follows natural-language directions.
terms.example: Turning a raw text-completion model into a helpful assistant.

terms.definition: A method that fine-tunes a model directly on chosen-versus-rejected response pairs, without training a separate reward model or running reinforcement learning.
terms.example: Training a model to prefer one of two candidate replies using a single classification-style loss.

terms.definition: The tendency of a neural network to overwrite previously learned general knowledge when it's fully fine-tuned on a new, narrow dataset.
terms.example: A model losing general conversational ability after heavy fine-tuning on legal documents.

terms.definition: A benchmark that measures a model's breadth of knowledge across 57 academic subjects using multiple-choice questions.
terms.example: Comparing two models' general knowledge scores across law, medicine, and STEM.

terms.definition: A benchmark of 164 Python programming problems that tests whether a model's generated code passes automated unit tests.
terms.example: Checking if a model's generated function correctly reverses a string.

terms.definition: Using a capable LLM to score or compare other models' outputs on subjective criteria like helpfulness or coherence, in place of human review.
terms.example: Asking a strong model to pick the better of two draft replies.

terms.definition: A curated set of test prompts and expected behaviors run automatically after every change to catch broken behavior before it reaches production.
terms.example: Re-running 200 saved prompts whenever a system prompt is edited.

terms.definition: A code-generation metric measuring how often at least one of k sampled solutions passes all tests for a given problem.
terms.example: pass@10 checks whether any of 10 sampled attempts solves the problem.

terms.definition: When a benchmark's questions and answers leak into a model's training data, inflating its score without real generalization.
terms.example: A model scoring suspiciously high because MMLU questions appeared in its training data.

terms.definition: The measurable benefit (cost savings, revenue, time saved) an AI initiative delivers relative to what it cost to build and run.
terms.example: A support chatbot that cuts ticket-handling time by 30%.

terms.definition: The full cost of an AI system over its lifetime, including compute, licensing, integration, and maintenance, not just the sticker price.
terms.example: API costs plus engineering time to integrate and monitor it.

terms.definition: The risk of becoming dependent on one AI provider's proprietary APIs or formats, making it costly to switch later.
terms.example: Building prompts and tooling that only work with one vendor's specific model quirks.

terms.definition: A small-scale, time-boxed trial of an AI use case used to validate value before a full rollout.
terms.example: Testing an AI drafting tool with one team for a month before a company-wide rollout.

terms.definition: A workflow design where a person reviews or approves AI output before it takes effect, balancing speed with accuracy and accountability.
terms.example: A human approving AI-drafted customer emails before they're sent.

terms.definition: The structured process of helping an organization adopt a new AI tool or workflow, covering training, communication, and incentives.
terms.example: Running workshops so staff trust and adopt a new AI assistant.

terms.definition: An AI assistant embedded in a workflow or tool that suggests or drafts work for a human to review and accept, rather than acting fully autonomously.
terms.example: A coding assistant suggesting the next line as you type.

terms.definition: A shared, reusable collection of vetted prompts an organization uses to get consistent results from an AI tool.
terms.example: A standard prompt template for summarizing meeting notes.

terms.definition: Practices ensuring sensitive or personal information isn't leaked to, stored by, or memorized by an AI system inappropriately.
terms.example: Redacting customer names before sending a support ticket to an AI summarizer.

terms.definition: Employees using AI tools without IT or security's knowledge or approval, creating unmanaged risk.
terms.example: Staff pasting confidential documents into a personal chatbot account.

terms.definition: Using AI to handle repetitive, multi-step tasks end-to-end within a business process, reducing manual work.
terms.example: An AI system that triages, drafts, and routes incoming support emails.

terms.definition: Training employees to effectively use and oversee AI tools as part of their role, rather than being replaced by them.
terms.example: Teaching a marketing team prompt-writing and AI output review skills.
